items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: Afghanistan: Weekly Market Report: Issue 298: Week 1 – June 2026
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: Afghanistan
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

Key Highlights

Exchange Rate and Trade Dynamics:

• During the first week of June, the Afghani exchange rate remained stable at AFN 63.8/USD, showing no change from the previous week. The Afghani continues to remain stronger than both last year (+9%) and the three-year average (+16%), helping to moderate imported inflationary pressures.

• Domestic markets continued to operate normally with generally adequate commodity availability, supported by ongoing domestic harvests, seasonal market arrivals, and sustained trade flows through regional corridors. However, transportation costs, regional trade uncertainties, and high dependence on imports continue to expose domestic supply chains to external market and geopolitical risks.

Food Items:

• Overall, national average food prices showed a generally stable trend during the first week of June, with slight week-on-week declines across major staple food items.

• Compared to last year, most food commodities continue to remain above their respective levels, particularly wheat grain (+12%), wheat flour (high-price +7%; low-price +10%), rice (high-quality +31%; low-quality +34%), cooking oil (+3%), sugar (+24%), and salt (+10%). Conversely, pulses (-12%) and bread (-4%) remained below last year's levels.

Vegetables:

• Vegetable prices continued to ease during the first week of June, largely driven by improved seasonal availability and increased domestic supply.

• Tomato prices recorded a significant weekly decline (-12.3%), continuing the downward trend observed in recent weeks. Onion and potato prices show negligible changes from last week, reflecting stable market conditions.

• Compared to last year, potato prices remain substantially above last year's levels (+44%), while tomato prices remain moderately higher (+6%). In contrast, onion prices continue to remain significantly below last year (-24%).

Non-Food Items:

• Diesel prices remained broadly stable during the week (+0.4%) and remains modestly above last year (+4%).

• Fertilizer prices (DAP and Urea) showed negligible week-on-week increases, while they remain significantly higher than last year, particularly DAP (+16%) and urea (+46%), continuing to exert pressure on agricultural production costs. Meanwhile, improved seed (-0.3%) and animal feed (-1.0%) recorded slight declines and remained relatively stable overall.

Livestock and Labour Market:

• The price of a one-year-old live female sheep declined by 6.8% during the week following elevated demand in the lead-up to Eid-ul-Adha. Despite the weekly decline, sheep prices remain broadly in line with last year and slightly above the three-year average.

• Labour market conditions remained weak. While the casual labour wage remained stable at AFN 304/day, labour availability declined to 1.9 days per week. Labour availability remains substantially below both last year (-20%) and the three-year average (-20%), highlighting continued challenges in income-earning opportunities for vulnerable households who are dependent on daily wage labour.

items.creator: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.title: Haiti: Haïti - Secteur Sécurité Alimentaire : Tableau de Bord de Suivi de la Réponse HNRP 2026 (janvier - mars 2026)
items.author: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.content:
Country: Haiti
Sources: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, Food Security Cluster, Government of Haiti, World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

APERÇU DE LA SITUATION :

De janvier à mars 2026, les différentes interventions des partenaires du secteur sécurité alimentaire ont permis d’atteindre 1,05 millions de personnes (bénéficiaires uniques), soit un taux de réalisation de 30% sur les 3,5 millions de personnes ciblées dans le plan de réponse HNRP.

Malgré des difficultés liées au contexte d'insécurité, les acteurs du secteur ont développé différentes stratégies pour assister les ménages affectés dans les différentes localités comme présenté ci-dessous.

La stratégie de ciblage est basée sur la réponse d’urgence aux personnes en IPC3 et plus et aux Personnes Déplacées Internes (PDIs).

L’assistance alimentaire d’urgence (CO1) a touché plus de 976 000 personnes soit 31% de la cible (3,2 M) à travers le bon d’achats/coupons et cash transfert (58%), en nature (42%) comme principale modalité d’intervention.

Les activités d'appui à la production alimentaire d’urgence (CO2) ont permis d’assister plus de 37 000 personnes à travers les modalités bon d’achats/coupons et cash transfert (4%) et en nature (96%).

Les activités de l’appui à la production agricole et aux moyens d’existence (CO3 et CO4) ont atteint 67 375 personnes, soit 5% de la cible (1,3 M) à travers 90% en espèces/cash ou bon d'achat/ coupons et 10% en nature et prestation de services/support.

Au total 34 partenaires dont 14 organisations principales et 23 partenaires d’implémentation interviennent.

Country: Haiti
Sources: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, Food Security Cluster, Government of Haiti, World Food Programme

La stratégie de ciblage est basée sur la réponse d’urgence aux personnes en IPC3 et plus et aux Personnes Déplacées Internes (PDIs).

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: World: CERF’s Climate Action Account (May 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: World
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

CERF’s Climate Action Account

Climate change is driving more frequent and severe disasters, escalating humanitarian needs worldwide. Vulnerable communities – particularly in Least Developed Countries, Small Island Developing States and countries affected by fragility, conflict and violence – bear the brunt of droughts, floods, and storms. Yet climate financing rarely reaches those communities.

The CERF Climate Action Account offers donors a high-impact opportunity to support faster, smarter, and more cost-effective humanitarian action. It upholds the life-saving mandate of the CERF (Resolution 60/124) whilst also aligning with the commitment to deliver humanitarian assistance that builds climate resilience (Resolutions 69/240 and 70/1). It channels additional climate-related financing to support climate action in crisis settings that otherwise would not occur. In doing so, the Account represents a step change in how CERF responds to the climate crisis, driving critical actions to:

  • Support humanitarian activities that build communities’ adaptive capacity,
  • Strengthen and scale anticipatory action,
  • Boost climate-smart humanitarian response, and
  • Foster innovation and advance best practice.

This fills a critical gap in the climate financing architecture. The CERF Climate Action Account operates efficiently and effectively, by leveraging CERF’s…

  • Unparalleled speed,
  • Operational expertise and reach in complex crises,
  • Robust, needs based allocation criteria,
  • Strategic targeting, and
  • Investment in coordinated, cost-effective and principled humanitarian action.

Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: World: CERF Annual Results Report 2025: Report on 2025 CERF-funded Climate Action
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Countries: World, Afghanistan, Bolivia (Plurinational State of), Cuba, Guatemala, Haiti, Jamaica, Mozambique, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines, Somalia, South Sudan, Sri Lanka, Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of), Viet Nam, Zambia
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Letter from the Emergency Relief Coordinator — Report on 2025 CERF-funded Climate Action

Around the world, the climate crisis is increasingly a humanitarian crisis—hitting hardest those who have done least to cause it.

In 2025, storms, floods and droughts once again devastated some of the world’s most vulnerable communities. Hurricane Melissa tore through the Caribbean as one of the most powerful storms ever recorded in the region, killing dozens and leaving widespread destruction across Jamaica, Haiti, the Dominican Republic and Cuba—just a year after Hurricane Beryl, compounding the hardship for communities still recovering. Severe droughts in Somalia and Afghanistan destroyed harvests, while floods in Nigeria, Pakistan and Venezuela overwhelmed already fragile systems.

For families caught up in these crises, these are not isolated shocks. They are blow after blow after blow.

There is no humanitarian solution to climate change.

That requires urgent global action—to cut emissions, accelerate adaptation, and address loss and damage. But humanitarian action can help people survive today’s crises and prepare for tomorrow’s.

CERF—the Central Emergency Response Fund—is doing exactly that. In 2025, it allocated $98 million to climate-related emergencies, reaching more than 5.5 million people across 16 countries affected by droughts, floods and storms.

CERF is also expanding anticipatory action—releasing funding based on forecasts and early warnings before disasters strike. In the Philippines, funding was released within minutes of storm thresholds being reached, enabling life-saving support to reach communities ahead of landfall. Similar action helped reduce the impact of drought in Guatemala and Afghanistan, and flooding in Nigeria.

This is humanitarian action at its best: faster, smarter, and more cost-effective.

At the same time, the CERF Climate Action Account is closing a critical gap in fragile and conflict-affected settings that often struggle to access climate finance. In 2025, it allocated $9.5 million across eight countries—supporting life-saving action today while helping communities withstand future shocks.

To all those who support CERF and the Climate Action Account: thank you.

We know what works. With sustained and predictable support, we can act earlier, reach more people, and ensure that the most vulnerable are not left behind.

Tom Fletcher

Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs
and Emergency Relief Coordinator

items.creator: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East
items.title: UNRWA Situation Report #12 on the Lebanon Emergency Response 2026 (4 June 2026) [EN/AR]
items.author: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East
items.content:
Countries: Lebanon, occupied Palestinian territory
Source: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East

Please refer to the attached files.

All information is valid for 20 May - 2 June 2026

Key Points

  • A 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon came into effect on 16 April. It was extended by three weeks on 23 April and again by 45 days on 15 May, bringing the current end date to mid-June. US-facilitated political negotiations continued on 2 June at the State Department in Washington, with the parties reviewing progress and working toward further extending the ceasefire.
  • The humanitarian situation in Lebanon remains fragile and unpredictable, with hostilities continuing despite the ceasefire, alongside periodic escalations, including airstrikes, evacuation warnings, and new displacement, particularly in the south of the country.
  • UNRWA launched its emergency response in Lebanon on 4 March. Since then, the Agency has been operating two collective emergency shelters[1]: Siblin Training Centre in Saida Area and Battir School in Nahr el-Bared Camp in northern Lebanon.
  • By 2 June, a total of 2,148 displaced people from 661 families were registered in the two UNRWA emergency shelters. This represents a significant increase from the 1,264 people recorded in the previous reporting period and follows the issuance of evacuation warnings and increased intensity of military activity, particularly south of the Litani River.
  • Between 20 May and 2 June, no security incidents affecting on-duty UNRWA personnel or facilities were reported.

Overall Situation

  • By 26 May, the Ministry of Social Affairs had documented the displacement of over 1.1 million individuals[2], including 127,714 people in 631 shelters[3]. Monitoring returns remains challenging due to the fluidity of population movements.
  • On 27 May, Israeli forces issued evacuation warnings for all areas south of the Zahrani River. This included areas such as Tyre City, which has three adjacent Palestine Refugee camps, as well as Adloun and Sarafand, where Palestine Refugees reside.
  • Airstrikes on 25 and 28 May targeted the areas near the Palestine Refugee camps of Rashidieh and El Buss. Combined with evacuation warnings, this resulted in a significant exodus from the camps, with around one-third of the camps’ usual population of 28,000 estimated to have left. Most displaced families seek shelter with relatives or host communities.
  • During the reporting period, Hezbollah continued to launch drones and rockets into northern Israel. On 30 May alone, Israeli authorities reported that over 25 projectiles were launched from Lebanon into Israel.
  • In a statement on 1 June, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said he had instructed Israeli forces to strike targets in Beirut. In reaction, large numbers of people left the southern suburbs of the city.
items.contentSnippet:
Countries: Lebanon, occupied Palestinian territory
Source: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East

Please refer to the attached files.
All information is valid for 20 May - 2 June 2026
Key Points
A 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon came into effect on 16 April. It was extended by three weeks on 23 April and again by 45 days on 15 May, bringing the current end date to mid-June. US-facilitated political negotiations continued on 2 June at the State Department in Washington, with the parties reviewing progress and working toward further extending the ceasefire.
The humanitarian situation in Lebanon remains fragile and unpredictable, with hostilities continuing despite the ceasefire, alongside periodic escalations, including airstrikes, evacuation warnings, and new displacement, particularly in the south of the country.
UNRWA launched its emergency response in Lebanon on 4 March. Since then, the Agency has been operating two collective emergency shelters[1]: Siblin Training Centre in Saida Area and Battir School in Nahr el-Bared Camp in northern Lebanon.
By 2 June, a total of 2,148 displaced people from 661 families were registered in the two UNRWA emergency shelters. This represents a significant increase from the 1,264 people recorded in the previous reporting period and follows the issuance of evacuation warnings and increased intensity of military activity, particularly south of the Litani River.
Between 20 May and 2 June, no security incidents affecting on-duty UNRWA personnel or facilities were reported.
Overall Situation
By 26 May, the Ministry of Social Affairs had documented the displacement of over 1.1 million individuals[2], including 127,714 people in 631 shelters[3]. Monitoring returns remains challenging due to the fluidity of population movements.
On 27 May, Israeli forces issued evacuation warnings for all areas south of the Zahrani River. This included areas such as Tyre City, which has three adjacent Palestine Refugee camps, as well as Adloun and Sarafand, where Palestine Refugees reside.
Airstrikes on 25 and 28 May targeted the areas near the Palestine Refugee camps of Rashidieh and El Buss. Combined with evacuation warnings, this resulted in a significant exodus from the camps, with around one-third of the camps’ usual population of 28,000 estimated to have left. Most displaced families seek shelter with relatives or host communities.
During the reporting period, Hezbollah continued to launch drones and rockets into northern Israel. On 30 May alone, Israeli authorities reported that over 25 projectiles were launched from Lebanon into Israel.
In a statement on 1 June, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said he had instructed Israeli forces to strike targets in Beirut. In reaction, large numbers of people left the southern suburbs of the city.
categories: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East

items.creator: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East
items.title: oPt: UNRWA Situation Report #224 on the Humanitarian Crisis in the Gaza Strip and the West Bank, including East Jerusalem [EN/AR]
items.author: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East

Please refer to the attached files.

All information updated for 26 May - 2 June 2026 [1]
Days 961 - 968 since the start of the war in the Gaza Strip

Highlights

The Gaza Strip

  • The Israeli government has instructed Israeli forces to expand military control to up to 70 per cent of the Gaza Strip. Current control is estimated at approximately 65 per cent, including the so-called “Orange Area.” Further expansion of the militarised area would likely trigger large-scale displacement, exacerbate overcrowding in other parts of the Gaza Strip, and significantly constrain humanitarian access — directly undermining UNRWA operations. Currently, 126 UNRWA facilities are located within the Israeli-militarised zone behind the so-called “Yellow Line”, as well as in areas within the “Orange Line” where access is subject to Israeli approval or coordination.
  • While Palestinians observed Eid Al-Adha on 27 May, Israeli forces intensified airstrikes across all areas of the Gaza Strip. They have also been issuing evacuation orders with increasing frequency. On 31 May, the roof of an UNRWA school-turned-shelter in Nuseirat was struck by a rocket, causing a breach in the upper rooftop structure, but no injuries were reported.
  • Severe shortages of engine oil, tires, and spare parts are placing life-saving humanitarian operations at risk across the Gaza Strip. Health services are particularly affected, as most facilities rely on generators that face frequent breakdowns. At the same time, water production, pumping and distribution systems — alongside wastewater and solid waste management — are at imminent risk of failure, as they depend almost entirely on ageing generators that are operating beyond design limits. Approximately 25 per cent of UNRWA vehicles — including water tankers and waste collection trucks — and 25 per cent of UNRWA generators are already out of service. Without urgent resupply, disruptions to healthcare, safe water access, and waste collection are expected to rapidly escalate public health risks and operational constraints.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East

Please refer to the attached files.
All information updated for 26 May - 2 June 2026 [1]
Highlights
The Gaza Strip
The Israeli government has instructed Israeli forces to expand military control to up to 70 per cent of the Gaza Strip. Current control is estimated at approximately 65 per cent, including the so-called “Orange Area.” Further expansion of the militarised area would likely trigger large-scale displacement, exacerbate overcrowding in other parts of the Gaza Strip, and significantly constrain humanitarian access — directly undermining UNRWA operations. Currently, 126 UNRWA facilities are located within the Israeli-militarised zone behind the so-called “Yellow Line”, as well as in areas within the “Orange Line” where access is subject to Israeli approval or coordination.
While Palestinians observed Eid Al-Adha on 27 May, Israeli forces intensified airstrikes across all areas of the Gaza Strip. They have also been issuing evacuation orders with increasing frequency. On 31 May, the roof of an UNRWA school-turned-shelter in Nuseirat was struck by a rocket, causing a breach in the upper rooftop structure, but no injuries were reported.
Severe shortages of engine oil, tires, and spare parts are placing life-saving humanitarian operations at risk across the Gaza Strip. Health services are particularly affected, as most facilities rely on generators that face frequent breakdowns. At the same time, water production, pumping and distribution systems — alongside wastewater and solid waste management — are at imminent risk of failure, as they depend almost entirely on ageing generators that are operating beyond design limits. Approximately 25 per cent of UNRWA vehicles — including water tankers and waste collection trucks — and 25 per cent of UNRWA generators are already out of service. Without urgent resupply, disruptions to healthcare, safe water access, and waste collection are expected to rapidly escalate public health risks and operational constraints.

items.creator: El Equipo Humanitario País Colombia
items.title: Colombia: Reporte Plan de Respuesta a Necesidades Humanitarias HNRP 2026, Sistema 5W - Beneficiarios ENE-ABR (28/05/2026)
items.author: El Equipo Humanitario País Colombia
items.content:
Country: Colombia
Sources: El Equipo Humanitario País Colombia, Grupo Interagencial sobre Flujos Migratorios Mixtos, R4V, UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Las organizaciones humanitarias siguen enfrentando desafíos del contexto, para alcanzar a la población en necesidades con la respuesta humanitaria complementaria. Es así como:

  • Entre enero y abril de 2026, más de 307.000 personas se beneficiaron en 197 municipios de 27 departamentos de Colombia por 43 socios humanitarios -principales e implementadores-. De este total, 197.000 beneficiarios (64% de los atendidos) ubicados en 90 municipios de 14 departamentos priorizados y clasificados en severidad 4, han recibido asistencia humanitaria complementaria en respuesta a las necesidades prolongadas y agudizadas. Hasta la fecha del reporte, estos beneficiarios recibieron atención en más de 2.100 actividades intersectoriales, en complementariedad a los esfuerzos de las autoridades e instituciones locales, y alineados al Plan de Respuesta a las Necesidades Humanitarias 2026 (HNRP 2026) y los Planes Territoriales departamentales-Comunitarios.
  • Otros 110.000 beneficiarios también fueron atendidos de manera más dispersa en 106 municipios de 26 departamentos clasificados con severidades 2 y 3. Hay que tener en cuenta que, las dinámicas del conflicto y violencia armada y los eventos asociados a la variabilidad climática se han expandido a nuevos territorios no priorizados en el HNRP 2026. Con base en lo anterior, se destacan municipios en Córdoba, Caquetá, Urabá, Amazonas, Valle del Cauca, Nariño y Guaviare, entre otros.
  • Según los reportes, un 36% de los beneficiarios atendidos corresponde a niños, niñas y adolescentes, mientras que un 18,4% corresponde a pueblos étnicos (9.9% afrodescendientes y 8.5% indígenas), principalmente en las regiones pacífico, noroccidente y nororiente (frontera con Venezuela), además de la región amazónica.
  • Gracias a la contribución de donantes y según el sistema FTS, se identificaron contribuciones por valor de casi USD 154,5 millones, cerca de un 54% del requerimiento total de USD 287 millones del HNRP 2026.
  • Las acciones implementadas por los socios de los Equipos Locales de Coordinación (ELC) y del Equipo Humanitario País (EHP) han sido llevadas a cabo principalmente por ONG internacionales (46%) y ONG nacionales y locales (38%)—estos datos incluyen tanto financiación directa como proyectos en los cuales estas son socios implementadores de Agencias del Naciones Unidas (5%)-, mientras que otro tipo de organizaciones han implementado de forma directa un 11%, según los reportes.
  • Las intervenciones continúan fortaleciendo comunidades e incluso organizaciones locales, con énfasis en protección (orientación legal y remisión gestión de casos con riesgo de protección); paquetes y asistencia con servicios de salud primarios y secundarios; entrega de insumos y kits esenciales de higiene, limpieza, potabilización para acceso al agua segura y comunicación para promover prácticas seguras; acciones de recuperación y mantenimiento de medios de subsistencia y activos productivos; asistencia alimentaria en especie y a través de transferencias monetarias; acciones de rehabilitación, recuperación y mantenimiento de los medios de subsistencia y activos productivos de comunidades priorizadas; además del fortalecimiento de capacidades pedagógicas, socioemocionales y de gestión de riesgo para docentes y agentes educativos, entre otras.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Colombia
Sources: El Equipo Humanitario País Colombia, Grupo Interagencial sobre Flujos Migratorios Mixtos, R4V, UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.
Las organizaciones humanitarias siguen enfrentando desafíos del contexto, para alcanzar a la población en necesidades con la respuesta humanitaria complementaria. Es así como:
Entre enero y abril de 2026, más de 307.000 personas se beneficiaron en 197 municipios de 27 departamentos de Colombia por 43 socios humanitarios -principales e implementadores-. De este total, 197.000 beneficiarios (64% de los atendidos) ubicados en 90 municipios de 14 departamentos priorizados y clasificados en severidad 4, han recibido asistencia humanitaria complementaria en respuesta a las necesidades prolongadas y agudizadas. Hasta la fecha del reporte, estos beneficiarios recibieron atención en más de 2.100 actividades intersectoriales, en complementariedad a los esfuerzos de las autoridades e instituciones locales, y alineados al Plan de Respuesta a las Necesidades Humanitarias 2026 (HNRP 2026) y los Planes Territoriales departamentales-Comunitarios.
Otros 110.000 beneficiarios también fueron atendidos de manera más dispersa en 106 municipios de 26 departamentos clasificados con severidades 2 y 3. Hay que tener en cuenta que, las dinámicas del conflicto y violencia armada y los eventos asociados a la variabilidad climática se han expandido a nuevos territorios no priorizados en el HNRP 2026. Con base en lo anterior, se destacan municipios en Córdoba, Caquetá, Urabá, Amazonas, Valle del Cauca, Nariño y Guaviare, entre otros.
Según los reportes, un 36% de los beneficiarios atendidos corresponde a niños, niñas y adolescentes, mientras que un 18,4% corresponde a pueblos étnicos (9.9% afrodescendientes y 8.5% indígenas), principalmente en las regiones pacífico, noroccidente y nororiente (frontera con Venezuela), además de la región amazónica.
Gracias a la contribución de donantes y según el sistema FTS, se identificaron contribuciones por valor de casi USD 154,5 millones, cerca de un 54% del requerimiento total de USD 287 millones del HNRP 2026.
Las acciones implementadas por los socios de los Equipos Locales de Coordinación (ELC) y del Equipo Humanitario País (EHP) han sido llevadas a cabo principalmente por ONG internacionales (46%) y ONG nacionales y locales (38%)—estos datos incluyen tanto financiación directa como proyectos en los cuales estas son socios implementadores de Agencias del Naciones Unidas (5%)-, mientras que otro tipo de organizaciones han implementado de forma directa un 11%, según los reportes.
Las intervenciones continúan fortaleciendo comunidades e incluso organizaciones locales, con énfasis en protección (orientación legal y remisión gestión de casos con riesgo de protección); paquetes y asistencia con servicios de salud primarios y secundarios; entrega de insumos y kits esenciales de higiene, limpieza, potabilización para acceso al agua segura y comunicación para promover prácticas seguras; acciones de recuperación y mantenimiento de medios de subsistencia y activos productivos; asistencia alimentaria en especie y a través de transferencias monetarias; acciones de rehabilitación, recuperación y mantenimiento de los medios de subsistencia y activos productivos de comunidades priorizadas; además del fortalecimiento de capacidades pedagógicas, socioemocionales y de gestión de riesgo para docentes y agentes educativos, entre otras.
categories: Grupo Interagencial sobre Flujos Migratorios Mixtos

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Lebanon: Flash Update #33 - Escalation of hostilities in Lebanon (as of 8 June 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • Hostilities continued with airstrikes and five new displacement orders covering 17 localities across Bekaa, the South and Nabatieh governorates triggering additional displacement.
  • At least 3,613 deaths and 11,072 injuries due to hostilities recorded by the Ministry of Public Health since 2 March.
  • A total of 134,800 Internally Displaced Persons were registered in 639 collective shelters on 8 June.
  • Over 12.6 million hot and cold meals distributed by Food Security and Agriculture Sector partners since 2 March.
  • 249,505 IDPs and other affected people have benefitted from protection activities, of whom 62 per cent women and girls, as of 8 June.
  • WASH assistance reached over 1 million internally displaced persons across collective shelters, host communities, and out-of-shelter settings.
  • Government report on the first three months of response highlights critical shortfalls in response capacity, noting reduced funding and in-kind contributions compared to 2024.

SITUATION OVERVIEW

Hostilities continued between 5 and 8 June, with new large-scale population movements attributable to displacement orders and/or airstrikes, including the airstrike on Beirut’s southern suburbs on 7 June. At least five displacement orders were issued during the reporting period affecting 17 localities across 5 districts of the South, Nabatieh and Bekaa Governorates.

According to the national Disaster Risk Management (DRM) unit, approximately 4,000 families were displaced in Nabatieh governorate and 30,000 in South governorate (representing around 85 per cent of those remaining) on 7 June. Preliminary estimates also indicated approximately 50,000 families, or around 200,000 people, were displaced from Beirut's southern suburbs on the same day. The majority of displaced families stayed in open spaces or in temporary tents while a small number moved to collective shelters in North governorate.

The renewed population movements continued to put increased pressure on already overstretched collective shelters and host communities. As 8 June, 134,800 people (35,600 families) were registered in 639 collective shelters. Local authorities and humanitarian partners reported that two collective shelters in two localities were temporarilyevacuated during the reporting period ue to displacement orders. Collective shelters in Beirut, Tyre, and Saida are at full capacity, while shelters in North governorate have open capacity. Authorities in Beirut and the North are exploring erecting additional tented accommodation to absorb continuing arrivals.

According to the Ministry of Public Health (MoPH), at least 3,613 people have been killed (including 344 women and 245 children) and 11,072 injured (including 1,360 women and 967 children) due to hostilities since 2 March. Threats and attacks impacting healthcare continue to be reported, with 201 attacks, resulting in 134 deaths among on-duty healthcare workers and 391 injuries recorded between 2 March and 8 June 2026.

The Presidency of the Council of Ministers has published a Comprehensive Response Report (March-May 2026) detailing the Lebanese Government's national emergency response to the escalation of hostilities that began on 2 March 2026 and which led to the internal displacement of approximately one million people. The report outlines immediate actions taken by the Government, including activating National Emergency Operations Room and deploying a tiered coordination architecture spanning national, sub-national, and local levels, drawing on a pre-existing National Preparedness Plan to rapidly open collective shelters within the first 72 hours.

The report also features the multisectoral response encompassing food security, health, WASH, shelter, education, protection, and social stability, supported by real-time digital dashboards tracking displacement, aid distribution, and in-kind donation flows to ensure transparency and accountability. The report highlights the critical funding shortfall compared to the 2024 response, with the initial 2026 Flash Appeal (March-May) achieving only 60.3 per cent coverage (USD 185.9 million of USD 308.3 million requested) against a 94.9 per cent coverage rate in 2024, and in-kind donations received at only 18 per cent of the volume recorded during the previous conflict period — underscoring the growing risk that donor fatigue and shifting global priorities pose to the continuity and scale of humanitarian assistance as needs remain acute and displacement unresolved.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Democratic republic of the Congo - Eastern DRC: Incidents Against Humanitarian Workers (April 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

The number of incidents affecting humanitarian actors in eastern DRC fell from 33 in March 2026 to 29 in April, a decrease of 12.1 per cent. Of these 29 incidents, 12 (41%) occurred in North Kivu, eight (28%) in South Kivu, six (21%) in Ituri and three (10%) in Tanganyika, while no incident was reported in Maniema. This decline suggests a relative improvement in the operating environment compared with February and March, although North Kivu continued to account for the largest share of reported incidents. In April, no humanitarian worker was killed or kidnapped, while one was injured.

Compared with the cumulative distribution of incidents recorded since January 2026, April 2026 showed a higher proportion of incidents in Ituri (19% versus 12%), while South Kivu accounted for a smaller share (26% versus 32%) as did Tanganyika (10% versus 18%). North Kivu remained broadly in line with the cumulative trend (39%). This suggests a relative deterioration of the security situation affecting humanitarian workers in Ituri compared with other provinces.

With regard to the incident-type chart in April, burglary, robbery and intrusions remained the largest category, representing 35 % of incidents recorded since January 2026. They were followed by interference and restrictions (32%), intimidation, threats or physical aggression (28%), and other incidents (4%). This continues to point to a risk environment shaped by criminality, operational interference and direct pressure on humanitarian personnel and assets.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: DR Congo: République démocratique du Congo : Aperçu des incidents affectant les travailleurs humanitaires (janvier à avril 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Le nombre d’incidents affectant les acteurs humanitaires dans l’est de la RDC est passé de 33 en mars 2026 à 29 en avril, soit une diminution de 12,1 %. Parmi ces 29 incidents, 12 (41 %) ont eu lieu au Nord-Kivu, 8 (28 %) au Sud-Kivu, 6 (21 %) en Ituri et 3 (10 %) dans le Tanganyika, tandis qu’aucun incident n’a été signalé au Maniema. Cette baisse indique une amélioration relative de l’environnement opérationnel par rapport aux mois de février et mars, bien que la plus grande part des incidents signalés reste concentrée dans le Nord-Kivu. En avril 2026, aucun travailleur humanitaire n’a été tué ou enlevé, toutefois, un humanitaire a été blessé.

Comparé au cumul des incidents enregistrés depuis janvier 2026, le mois d’avril 2026 a été marqué par une proportion plus élevée d’incidents en Ituri (19 % contre 12 %), tandis que le Sud-Kivu a connu une diminution (26 % contre 32 %) de même que le Tanganyika (10 % contre 18 %). En revanche, dans le Nord-Kivu la tendance est restée conforme à la tendance cumulative (39 %). Cela indique une détérioration relative de la situation sécuritaire affectant les travailleurs humanitaires en Ituri par rapport aux autres provinces.

Concernant la typologie des incidents en avril, les cambriolages, vols et intrusions sont restés la catégorie la plus importante, représentant 35 % des incidents enregistrés depuis janvier 2026. Ils sont suivis par les interférences et restrictions (32 %), l’intimidation, les menaces ou agressions physiques (28 %), et les autres incidents (4 %). Cette répartition illustre un environnement à risque marqué par la criminalité, les interférences opérationnelles et la pression directe sur le personnel et les biens humanitaires.

items.creator: Famine Early Warning System Network
items.title: World: Food Assistance Outlook Brief, June 2026
items.author: Famine Early Warning System Network
items.content:
Countries: World, Afghanistan, Burkina Faso, Burundi, Cameroon, Central African Republic, Chad, Democratic Republic of the Congo, El Salvador, Guatemala, Haiti, Honduras, Kenya, Lebanon, Madagascar, Malawi, Mali, Mozambique, Niger, Nigeria, Somalia, South Sudan, Sudan, Syrian Arab Republic, Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of), Yemen, Zimbabwe
Source: Famine Early Warning System Network

Please refer to the attached file.

Key messages

In December 2026, FEWS NET expects the number of people in need of humanitarian food assistance to be highest in the countries of Sudan, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Nigeria, and Yemen, followed by South Sudan.

When food assistance needs are expressed as a percent of the total population of each respective country, FEWS NET expects the share of the population that needs humanitarian food assistance to be highest in South Sudan, where 50-55 percent of the country’s population will most likely need food assistance. The share of the country’s population that needs food assistance is also very high in Sudan (40-45 percent), Yemen (35-40 percent), and Somalia, Haiti, and Lebanon (25-30 percent each).

Out of the projected total 115-125 million people in need across FEWS NET’s presence countries, Sudan, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Nigeria, and Yemen are each expected to contribute over 10 percent of total food assistance needs. South Sudan and Afghanistan are expected to contribute 5-10 percent of total food assistance needs.

In comparison to December 2025, FEWS NET expects the number of people in need of food assistance to be higher in Nigeria, South Sudan, Somalia, and Lebanon. In contrast, FEWS NET expects the number of people in need of food assistance to be lower in Afghanistan, Kenya, Mozambique, Malawi, and Venezuela. In all other countries, food assistance needs are expected to be similar to last year.

items.creator: Centre for Sustainable Peace and Democratic Development
items.title: Ukraine: Відбудова Миколаївської області: шляхи до стійкості та відновлення - Результати дослідження reSCORE у 2024 році [UK]
items.author: Centre for Sustainable Peace and Democratic Development
items.content:
Country: Ukraine
Source: Centre for Sustainable Peace and Democratic Development

Please refer to the attached file.

Ключові висновки

Як регіон на лінії фронту, Миколаївська область долає численні виклики внаслідок повномасштабного вторгнення, яке досі триває. Для цього було мобілізовано фінансові ресурси та реалізовано низку інтервенцій, спрямованих на підтримку процесів відбудови соціального життя та локальної економіки, а також інфраструктури Миколаївської області. Звіт має на меті визначити ключові напрями, за якими місцева влада та організації, які займаються питаннями розвитку, можуть сприяти посиленню стійкості регіону, пришвидшенню процесів відбудови та загальній відбудові в умовах тривалої кризи.

Source: Centre for Sustainable Peace and Democratic Development

items.creator: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.title: oPt: West Bank and Gaza Strip: FAO Humanitarian response update, June 2026
items.author: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

Please refer to the attached file.

• Under the 2026 Flash Appeal, the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) requires USD 72.5 million (USD 52.9 million for the Gaza Strip and USD 19.6 million for the West Bank). This will enable FAO to support 30 500 farming households (161 000 people) with conditional cash and targeted in-kind assistance to protect and reactivate food production. To date, only USD 2.1 million has been received.

• FAO has also developed a ten-year Recovery and Reconstruction Programme (2026–2036) for the Gaza Strip, with a total required budget of USD 300 million. For the first three-year phase, USD 90 million is needed to provide conditional cash transfers to 12 000 farming households. Subsequent phases will support the restoration of productive assets through co-investment grants, the reconstruction of agrifood market infrastructure and facilities, and the rehabilitation of public and private services.

• FAO has contributed to three UN joint projects with multiple agencies under the UN Palestine Recovery Trust Fund for the Gaza Strip.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

Please refer to the attached file.
• Under the 2026 Flash Appeal, the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) requires USD 72.5 million (USD 52.9 million for the Gaza Strip and USD 19.6 million for the West Bank). This will enable FAO to support 30 500 farming households (161 000 people) with conditional cash and targeted in-kind assistance to protect and reactivate food production. To date, only USD 2.1 million has been received.
• FAO has also developed a ten-year Recovery and Reconstruction Programme (2026–2036) for the Gaza Strip, with a total required budget of USD 300 million. For the first three-year phase, USD 90 million is needed to provide conditional cash transfers to 12 000 farming households. Subsequent phases will support the restoration of productive assets through co-investment grants, the reconstruction of agrifood market infrastructure and facilities, and the rehabilitation of public and private services.
• FAO has contributed to three UN joint projects with multiple agencies under the UN Palestine Recovery Trust Fund for the Gaza Strip.
